title: IT Service Management Awareness
description: An introductory module covering the fundamentals of IT service management, the service lifecycle, service level agreements, the service catalogue, and the relationship between ITSM and business value.

sections.title: What Is IT Service Management?
sections.content:
IT Service Management (ITSM) is the set of policies, processes, and procedures used by an organisation to design, deliver, manage, and improve the IT services it provides to its customers and users. ISO 20000-1 is the international standard that specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving a service management system (SMS). It provides a structured framework for ensuring that IT services are aligned with business needs and delivered to an agreed level of quality.

ITSM moves beyond a purely technical view of IT and places the focus on the delivery of value to the business and its customers. Rather than managing individual technologies or systems in isolation, ITSM treats IT as a collection of services, each designed to support specific business outcomes. This service-oriented approach ensures that IT investments are directed towards activities that deliver the greatest benefit.

The adoption of ITSM practices brings numerous benefits, including improved service quality and reliability, greater alignment between IT and business objectives, enhanced customer and user satisfaction, more efficient use of resources, and a structured approach to managing risk and change. Organisations that implement ISO 20000-1 demonstrate a commitment to service excellence that is recognised internationally.

IT Service Management is relevant to organisations of all sizes and across all sectors. Whether the IT function serves internal users within a single organisation or provides services to external customers on a commercial basis, the principles and practices of ITSM provide a sound foundation for effective service delivery.

sections.title: The Service Lifecycle
sections.content:
The service lifecycle describes the stages through which an IT service passes from its initial conception through to its eventual retirement. Understanding the service lifecycle is essential for managing services effectively and for ensuring that they continue to meet business requirements throughout their operational life.

The lifecycle typically begins with service strategy, during which the organisation determines which services to offer, to whom, and at what level. This phase involves understanding customer needs, assessing market opportunities, and making investment decisions. Service design follows, translating strategic requirements into detailed service specifications, including the processes, technology, and resources needed to deliver the service.

Service transition covers the activities involved in building, testing, and deploying new or changed services into the live environment. This phase is critical for managing the risks associated with change and for ensuring that new services perform as expected. Service operation focuses on the day-to-day management of live services, including incident resolution, request fulfilment, and monitoring of service performance.

Finally, continual service improvement provides the mechanisms for evaluating service performance and for identifying and implementing improvements. This phase ensures that services evolve in line with changing business needs and that lessons learned from operational experience are fed back into the earlier stages of the lifecycle. The service lifecycle is iterative, with feedback loops connecting each phase to the others.

sections.title: Service Level Agreements
sections.content:
A Service Level Agreement (SLA) is a formal, documented agreement between the service provider and the customer that defines the level of service to be delivered. SLAs are a cornerstone of IT service management, as they establish clear expectations, provide a basis for measuring performance, and create accountability for service delivery.

An effective SLA typically specifies the services covered by the agreement, the performance targets and metrics (such as availability, response times, and resolution times), the responsibilities of both the service provider and the customer, the reporting and review arrangements, and the procedures for managing exceptions and escalations. SLAs should be written in clear, unambiguous language that is understood by both technical and non-technical stakeholders.

The process of agreeing SLAs requires open and constructive dialogue between the service provider and the customer. It is important that SLA targets are realistic and achievable, as setting targets that cannot be met undermines confidence in the service and in the management system. Conversely, targets that are too easily achieved may not drive the level of performance that the business requires.

SLAs should be reviewed and updated on a regular basis to reflect changes in business requirements, service capabilities, and the operating environment. Performance against SLA targets should be monitored and reported on an ongoing basis, with formal reviews conducted at agreed intervals. Where SLA targets are not being met, the service provider should investigate the causes and take appropriate corrective action.

sections.title: The Service Catalogue
sections.content:
The service catalogue is a comprehensive, maintained database or document that contains information about all live IT services, including those available for deployment. It serves as the definitive source of information about the services that the IT function provides and is a key communication tool between the service provider and its customers and users.

A well-maintained service catalogue provides clarity about what services are available, who they are available to, and how they can be accessed. For each service, the catalogue typically includes a description of the service, the business outcomes it supports, the service hours and availability targets, the support arrangements, any costs or charging mechanisms, and the process for requesting the service.

The service catalogue may be presented in different views depending on the audience. A business service catalogue presents services in terms that are meaningful to customers and users, focusing on the outcomes and value delivered. A technical service catalogue provides more detailed information about the underlying technology, components, and dependencies, and is used by IT staff for management and support purposes.

Maintaining an accurate and up-to-date service catalogue requires a defined process and clear ownership. Services should be added, updated, or retired as the portfolio evolves. The service catalogue should be readily accessible to all stakeholders and should be promoted as the primary source of information about IT services. An effective service catalogue reduces confusion, supports demand management, and improves the overall customer experience.

sections.title: The Relationship Between ITSM and Business Value
sections.content:
The ultimate purpose of IT Service Management is to enable the delivery of value to the business and its customers. Value in this context is defined by the customer and is determined by the extent to which the services provided meet their needs, support their objectives, and contribute to the achievement of desired outcomes. ITSM provides the framework through which this value is consistently and reliably delivered.

The relationship between ITSM and business value operates at multiple levels. At the strategic level, ITSM ensures that IT investments are aligned with business priorities and that the service portfolio reflects the current and future needs of the organisation. At the operational level, ITSM processes such as incident management, problem management, and change management ensure that services are available, reliable, and responsive to user needs.

Demonstrating the business value of IT services requires effective measurement and reporting. Key performance indicators should be linked to business outcomes, not just technical metrics. For example, rather than reporting solely on system uptime, the IT function should also report on the impact of service availability on business operations, customer satisfaction, and revenue. This outcome-focused approach strengthens the relationship between IT and the business.

ISO 20000-1 supports the delivery of business value by providing a structured, auditable framework for service management. Certification provides assurance to customers, regulators, and other stakeholders that the organisation manages its IT services to an internationally recognised standard. This assurance, combined with the operational improvements that come from implementing the standard, creates a compelling case for ITSM as a strategic enabler of business success.

title: Incident & Problem Management
description: A focused module covering incident classification and prioritisation, escalation procedures, problem identification and root cause analysis, and the management of known errors and workarounds within an ISO 20000-1 IT service management system.

sections.title: Incident Classification and Prioritisation
sections.content:
An incident is an unplanned interruption to an IT service, or a reduction in the quality of an IT service. Incident management aims to restore normal service operation as quickly as possible and to minimise the adverse impact on business operations. Effective incident management depends upon the accurate classification and prioritisation of incidents to ensure that resources are directed where they are most needed.

Classification involves categorising each incident according to its nature, the service affected, the type of fault, and any other relevant attributes. A well-defined classification scheme enables the service desk and support teams to route incidents to the appropriate resolver groups, supports trend analysis and reporting, and contributes to problem management by highlighting recurring issues.

Prioritisation determines the order in which incidents are addressed, based on a combination of urgency and impact. Urgency reflects how quickly the incident needs to be resolved, whilst impact reflects the extent to which the incident affects the business. A priority matrix is commonly used to combine these two factors into a single priority level, which in turn determines the target response and resolution times.

Consistent and accurate classification and prioritisation are essential for maintaining confidence in the incident management process. All service desk and support staff should be trained in the use of the classification scheme and the priority matrix, and should understand the business rationale behind the prioritisation criteria. Regular review of the classification scheme ensures that it remains relevant and effective.

sections.title: Escalation Procedures
sections.content:
Escalation is the mechanism by which incidents that cannot be resolved within the target timeframe, or that exceed the capability or authority of the current resolver, are referred to a higher level of support or management. Effective escalation procedures are critical for ensuring that significant incidents receive the attention and resources they require.

There are two types of escalation: functional and hierarchical. Functional escalation, sometimes referred to as technical escalation, involves passing the incident to a team or individual with greater technical expertise. This is the normal route for incidents that require specialist knowledge or access to resolve. Hierarchical escalation, sometimes referred to as management escalation, involves notifying or involving management when an incident is likely to breach its SLA targets, when it has a significant business impact, or when a decision beyond the authority of the resolver is required.

Escalation procedures should be clearly documented and communicated to all support staff. They should define the criteria for escalation, the individuals or teams to whom incidents should be escalated, the information that must be provided at the point of escalation, and the expected response from the escalation recipient. Timely escalation prevents incidents from stagnating and ensures that the organisation's commitments to its customers are met.

It is important to foster a culture in which escalation is viewed positively, as a means of ensuring the best possible outcome for the customer, rather than as a sign of failure. Support staff should be encouraged to escalate without hesitation when the criteria are met, and management should respond promptly and constructively to escalated incidents.

sections.title: Problem Identification and Root Cause
sections.content:
A problem is the underlying cause of one or more incidents. Problem management aims to identify and resolve the root causes of incidents, thereby reducing the number and impact of future incidents. Whilst incident management focuses on restoring service as quickly as possible, problem management takes a longer-term view, seeking to prevent incidents from occurring or recurring.

Problems may be identified through several channels, including trend analysis of incident data, proactive monitoring of the infrastructure, reports from support staff who notice recurring patterns, and feedback from major incident reviews. A well-functioning problem management process actively seeks out problems rather than waiting for them to be reported.

Once a problem has been identified, the next step is to diagnose its root cause. Root cause analysis techniques such as the 5 Whys, Ishikawa diagrams, and fault tree analysis are commonly used in IT service management, just as they are in other disciplines. The analysis should be evidence-based and should involve individuals with the appropriate technical knowledge and experience.

The root cause analysis may reveal a need for changes to the infrastructure, applications, processes, documentation, or training. Any such changes should be managed through the change management process to ensure that they are assessed, approved, and implemented in a controlled manner. The ultimate goal of problem management is to eliminate the root cause and to prevent the associated incidents from recurring.

sections.title: Known Errors and Workarounds
sections.content:
A known error is a problem that has a documented root cause and, where applicable, a temporary workaround. The known error database (KEDB) is a repository of known errors and their workarounds, and is a valuable tool for both incident and problem management.

When a root cause has been identified but a permanent fix is not yet available, a workaround may be developed to mitigate the impact of the problem and to enable faster resolution of related incidents. Workarounds should be clearly documented, including any limitations or risks associated with their use, and should be made available to service desk and support staff through the KEDB.

The KEDB enables the service desk to resolve incidents more quickly by providing ready access to proven solutions and workarounds. When a user reports an incident that matches a known error, the service desk can apply the documented workaround without the need for further diagnosis, reducing the resolution time and improving the customer experience.

It is important that known errors are not left indefinitely without a permanent resolution. The problem management process should track all known errors and should ensure that permanent fixes are planned, prioritised, and implemented through the change management process. Regular review of the KEDB helps to ensure that workarounds remain effective and that obsolete entries are removed. Over time, the systematic resolution of known errors leads to a more stable and reliable service environment.

title: Change & Release Management
description: A practical module covering change types and assessment, the change advisory board, release planning, and deployment and post-implementation review within the context of ISO 20000-1 IT service management.

sections.title: Change Types and Assessment
sections.content:
Change management is the process responsible for controlling the lifecycle of all changes to the IT infrastructure, applications, and services. Its primary objective is to enable beneficial changes to be made with minimum disruption to services. ISO 20000-1 requires organisations to establish a formal change management process that ensures all changes are recorded, assessed, approved, implemented, and reviewed.

Changes are typically categorised by type to determine the appropriate level of assessment and approval. Standard changes are pre-authorised, low-risk changes that follow a well-understood procedure and do not require individual assessment. Normal changes follow the full assessment and approval process. Emergency changes are those that must be implemented urgently to restore service or to prevent an imminent threat, and follow an expedited process with retrospective documentation and review.

The assessment of a change involves evaluating its potential impact on services, infrastructure, and other changes; the risks associated with implementation and with not implementing the change; the resources required; the implementation plan and schedule; and the back-out plan in the event that the change fails. A thorough impact assessment is essential for informed decision making and for minimising the risk of unintended consequences.

All changes should be recorded in a change log or change management system, providing a complete audit trail from request through to closure. This record supports trend analysis, facilitates post-implementation review, and provides evidence of compliance with the change management process.

sections.title: The Change Advisory Board
sections.content:
The Change Advisory Board (CAB) is a body that exists to support the authorisation of changes and to assist change management in the assessment and prioritisation of changes. The CAB typically comprises representatives from IT, the business, and, where appropriate, external suppliers. Its composition may vary depending on the nature of the changes being considered.

The CAB's primary function is to review proposed changes and to provide advice on their approval, scheduling, and prioritisation. Members bring diverse perspectives and expertise, which helps to ensure that changes are assessed from multiple viewpoints and that potential impacts on different areas of the business are identified. The CAB does not typically have the authority to approve or reject changes itself; rather, it makes recommendations to the change authority, who is accountable for the decision.

CAB meetings are usually held on a regular schedule, with an agenda that includes a review of the forward schedule of change, an assessment of proposed normal changes, a review of emergency changes implemented since the last meeting, and a review of any failed or backed-out changes. The regularity and discipline of CAB meetings contribute to a well-managed change environment.

For emergency changes, a streamlined process is typically in place that allows a smaller group, sometimes referred to as the Emergency CAB (ECAB), to assess and authorise changes at short notice. Even in emergency situations, the principles of assessment, authorisation, and documentation should be maintained, with any shortcuts documented and addressed retrospectively.

sections.title: Release Planning
sections.content:
Release management is the process responsible for planning, scheduling, and controlling the movement of releases to test and live environments. A release is a collection of one or more changes to an IT service that are built, tested, and deployed together. Release management ensures that the integrity of the live environment is protected and that the correct components are deployed.

Release planning begins with the definition of the release scope, which specifies the changes and components to be included. The release plan should detail the activities required to build, test, and deploy the release; the resources needed; the schedule and milestones; the acceptance criteria; and the communication plan. Dependencies between releases and other changes should be identified and managed to avoid conflicts.

Testing is a critical element of release management. Each release should be tested in an environment that replicates the live environment as closely as possible, using test cases that verify both the intended functionality and the absence of unintended side effects. Testing should be conducted in accordance with a defined test plan and should be signed off before the release is approved for deployment.

Release planning should also include a back-out plan that defines the steps to be taken if the release fails or causes unacceptable disruption. The availability of a tested back-out plan provides confidence that the organisation can recover quickly if problems arise during deployment. The back-out plan should be as thoroughly tested as the release itself.

sections.title: Deployment and Post-Implementation Review
sections.content:
Deployment is the activity responsible for moving new or changed hardware, software, documentation, and processes into the live environment. The deployment process should follow the approved release plan and should be executed by appropriately skilled and authorised personnel. Communication with affected users and stakeholders before, during, and after deployment is essential for managing expectations and minimising disruption.

During deployment, the implementation team should monitor the progress of the release against the plan, verify that each step has been completed successfully, and be prepared to invoke the back-out plan if problems arise. A clear go/no-go decision point should be established, at which the deployment is assessed against the acceptance criteria before proceeding to full implementation.

The Post-Implementation Review (PIR) is conducted after the release has been deployed to assess whether the change achieved its intended objectives, whether it was implemented as planned, and whether any issues arose during or after deployment. The PIR provides valuable feedback that supports the improvement of both the change and release management processes.

Findings from the PIR should be documented and communicated to all relevant parties. Positive outcomes should be acknowledged, and any issues or lessons learned should be used to inform future changes and releases. Over time, a disciplined approach to deployment and post-implementation review builds a track record of successful change delivery and strengthens the organisation's confidence in its ability to evolve its services in a controlled and reliable manner.
